common_name: Common Monkey Flower (Yellow Monkey Flower, Seep Monkey Flower)

planting.note: Surface-sow seeds — do not cover, as they require light for germination — in cool, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade or beside water features; monkey flower grows naturally in seeps, wet meadows, and stream banks and requires consistent moisture to perform well.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade beside streams, ponds, or bog gardens; monkey flower is a widely distributed native perennial of the Americas producing cheerful, snapdragon-like yellow flowers spotted with reddish-brown at the throat from spring through fall as long as moisture is adequate. The flowers are genuinely designed for bumblebees, which squeeze the flower open to reach the nectar — an interaction called buzz pollination. Common monkey flower is among the most successful colonizers of disturbed wet habitats in its native western range and has naturalized throughout the eastern US in suitable moist spots. It spreads by stolons in wet conditions to form dense colonies at stream edges and wet seeps. In the garden it is outstanding in rain gardens, bog gardens, and at pond margins where its cheery yellow flowers add color through the season in partial to dappled shade. Plants may behave as perennials or self-seeding annuals depending on climate.

planting.note: Surface-sow seeds in cool, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade or start transplants indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost; scarlet monkey flower requires consistently wet soil and will not tolerate drought at any stage.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade beside water features, in bog gardens, or at moist streambanks; scarlet monkey flower is a spectacular western native wildflower producing intensely vivid scarlet-red, tubular flowers on upright stems that are outstanding hummingbird magnets — the elongated red tube is precisely designed for hummingbird pollination, with the stamens positioned to dust the bird's head with pollen as it feeds. Plants bloom from late spring through fall in cool, moist conditions. Like yellow monkey flower, it requires consistently wet soil and colonizes stream margins, seeps, and wet rocky canyon walls in its native western habitat from Oregon to Baja California. In the shade garden it is extraordinary for adding hummingbird-attracting red color in moist, shaded spots where most other red flowers fail. Excellent in containers kept standing in water in partial shade. Treat as a tender perennial in zone 9–10, short-lived perennial in zone 5–8.
sources: University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources

common_name: Cat's Whiskers (Java Tea, Kidney Tea Plant)

planting.note: Plant cuttings or transplants after all frost danger passes in warm (65°F+) soil in partial shade; in zone 9–11 cat's whiskers is a perennial that thrives in the humid summer conditions and partial shade where many herbs struggle. Easily propagated from tip cuttings rooted in moist potting mix.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; cat's whiskers is one of the most striking tropical herbs for zone 9–11 shade gardens, producing distinctive white or lavender flower spikes with extraordinarily long stamens that project like cat's whiskers beyond the petals — an unusual, eye-catching floral structure that attracts bees and butterflies. The plant is widely cultivated throughout Southeast Asia as a medicinal herb — Orthosiphon leaves are dried to make Java tea, a traditional remedy for kidney and urinary tract conditions. In the Gulf Coast garden it is an outstanding tender perennial for partial shade where it blooms almost continuously from late spring through frost in frost-free climates. The fresh or dried leaves make a mild, pleasant-tasting herbal infusion. Harvest leaves and young stems by pinching before they flower to encourage bushy growth and maximize leaf yield. Propagate easily from tip cuttings placed in moist soil in a warm, humid location. In zone 9 it dies back to the roots in frost but recovers vigorously in spring.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost in warm (70–80°F) conditions; transplant after all frost danger passes. In zone 9, plant in fall for a winter crop — ground cherry performs as a cool-season vegetable in the Gulf Coast South where it fruits prolifically through winter and spring before summer heat causes decline.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; ground cherry is an often-overlooked member of the Physalis family closely related to the tomatillo, producing small, marble-sized golden-yellow fruit enclosed in papery tan husks like a miniature tomatillo. The flavor is remarkable — simultaneously sweet, tropical, and slightly vanilla-like, unlike any other common garden vegetable. The plants are bushy and sprawling, reaching 2–3 feet high, and produce prodigiously once established. Fruit drops to the ground when ripe (hence the name), where it can be left in its husk for several weeks without deteriorating. Eat fresh, bake in desserts, make preserves, or dry for extended storage. In zone 9, treat as a cool-season annual planted in fall for winter through spring production. The husk protects the fruit from pests and makes storage easy. Self-seeds prolifically in warm soils and will naturalize in suitable areas. Not to be confused with the cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana), though they are closely related.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in dry, rocky, very well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; wild pink grows naturally in extremely well-drained, rocky or sandy woodland soils and will not survive in poorly drained sites.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild pink is a charming, mat-forming native perennial of the eastern Piedmont and Appalachians, producing clusters of vivid pink, 5-petaled flowers atop low, sticky-stemmed rosettes in mid-spring. The bright cerise-to-magenta flower color is outstanding in the late-spring garden and attracts butterflies. The common name 'sticky catchfly' refers to the sticky stems that trap small insects, though the plant is not carnivorous. Wild pink grows naturally on rocky ledges, sandy barrens, and dry oak-pine woodland edges where the soil drains rapidly and conditions alternate between exposed sun and light shade from scattered trees. It is extremely sensitive to poor drainage — in heavy clay or consistently wet soil it will quickly rot. An outstanding plant for rock gardens, dry shade under pines, and naturalized woodland edges in the Southeast. Self-seeds sparingly in appropriate conditions.

planting.note: Plant bare-root plugs or divisions in spring in wet, brackish to saline, sandy or muddy soil at the tidal marsh edge or in wet, salt-tolerant garden settings; saltmarsh cordgrass must have consistent moisture and tolerates flooding and salt spray that would kill most plants.

directions: Plant in consistently wet, brackish to saline, sandy or muddy soil in full sun at tidal marsh edges, coastal restoration sites, or wet, salt-influenced garden areas; saltmarsh cordgrass is the foundational native grass of Atlantic and Gulf Coast salt marshes, forming the vast meadows of gently waving green that define the coastal landscape from Maine to Texas. It tolerates full tidal flooding, saline soils, and constant wave action that no other ornamental grass can withstand, making it irreplaceable for coastal restoration and stabilization. The coarse, upright stems reach 2–6 feet and produce seed heads in late summer that feed coastal sparrows, rails, and other marsh birds. Rhizomatous roots spread to stabilize shorelines, prevent erosion, and trap sediments. Essential for living shoreline projects, coastal restoration, and saline wet-garden situations in zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens near the bay or marsh. Not appropriate for ordinary garden use but outstanding in naturalized coastal settings where its ecological function and native character make it irreplaceable.
sources: NOAA Office for Coastal Management — Coastal Resilience Plants
